ideas.Modes (tropes) of skepticism:
The "**modes**" or "**tropes**" (*tropoi*) of skepticism are the systematic argumentative strategies that Sextus Empiricus compiled and transmitted as the technical core of **Pyrrhonist** skepticism — organized sets of considerations designed to induce **suspension of judgment** (*epoché*) by showing that for any claim about the real nature of things, we can always oppose equally compelling considerations, leaving us unable to decide. These modes are among the most influential tools in the entire skeptical tradition.

Sextus records several such sets. The most famous are the "**Ten Modes**" (attributed to the earlier skeptic Aenesidemus), which marshal the **variability of appearances** to defeat any claim about how things really are. They point out, for example, that the same things appear differently to different **animals**, to different **humans**, to the different **senses**, in different **conditions** of the perceiver (sick or healthy, waking or dreaming), from different **positions, distances, and places**, in different **quantities**, in relation to other things, and according to different **customs and laws** among peoples. Since the same object presents contradictory appearances depending on all these variable circumstances, and we have no non-arbitrary way to privilege one appearance as revealing the *real* nature of the thing, we must suspend judgment about how it really is.

Sextus also records more compressed and abstract sets: the "**Five Modes**" (attributed to Agrippa), which are especially powerful and general — including the modes of **dispute** (unresolved disagreement), **infinite regress** (every proof requires a further proof), **relativity**, **hypothesis** (arbitrary unproven assumptions), and **circularity** (reciprocal proof). These five expose the impossibility of ever finally *justifying* any claim, since every attempt at proof either regresses infinitely, argues in a circle, or rests on an unjustified assumption — the famous "**Agrippa's trilemma**." By deploying these modes, the skeptic systematically brings about the suspension of judgment that leads to tranquility.
ideas.Suspension of judgment as therapy:
A central and distinctive theme in Sextus Empiricus's presentation of **Pyrrhonism** is the understanding of skepticism — and especially its culmination in **suspension of judgment** (*epoché*) — as a form of **therapy**, a cure for the psychological disturbance caused by dogmatic belief. For Sextus, the skeptic is not a cold theorist bent on refutation for its own sake, but something more like a **physician of the soul**, and the goal of the whole skeptical enterprise is not knowledge but **tranquility** (*ataraxia*).

Sextus explains the skeptic's path with a memorable diagnosis. The skeptic *began*, like everyone, troubled by the conflicts and contradictions among appearances and beliefs, and hoped to attain peace by *resolving* them — by discovering which claims are true. Investigating, however, the skeptic found only *equipollence* (*isostheneia*): for every argument, an equally strong opposing argument, so that no question about the real nature of things could be decided. Unable to decide, the skeptic **suspended judgment** — and then, unexpectedly, found that **tranquility followed** upon this suspension, "as a shadow follows a body." The very peace that had been sought through knowledge came instead through the *abandonment* of the quest for it.

The disturbance, Sextus argues, comes precisely from **dogmatism**: the person who holds firm beliefs about what is really good and bad is perpetually anxious — agitated in pursuing supposed goods and avoiding supposed evils, and distressed when things go against his convictions. The skeptic, by suspending judgment on all such matters and simply following appearances and custom in daily life without dogmatic commitment, is freed from this turmoil. Sextus even compares the skeptic's arguments to **purgative drugs** that eliminate themselves along with the ailment, and describes skepticism as a medical art. This therapeutic conception — skepticism as a practice aimed at healing the soul and achieving peace — is one of Pyrrhonism's most profound and appealing features.
ideas.Critique of dogmatic philosophers:
A large part of Sextus Empiricus's surviving work consists of a sustained, detailed **critique of the "dogmatists"** — his term for the philosophers of the various schools (Stoics, Epicureans, Platonists, Aristotelians, and others) who claimed to have discovered the truth and asserted positive doctrines about the real nature of things. Sextus's skeptical method was largely a matter of turning the dogmatists' own tools against them, systematically dismantling their claims to knowledge across every branch of philosophy.

Sextus organized his assault along the standard divisions of Hellenistic philosophy — **logic** (or the theory of knowledge and reasoning), **physics** (natural philosophy, including theology and causation), and **ethics**. In each domain, he marshaled the skeptical **modes** and a battery of specific arguments to show that the dogmatists' cherished doctrines are unfounded and that opposing views are equally defensible, so that suspension of judgment is the only reasonable outcome. He attacked, for example, the dogmatic accounts of the criterion of truth, of proof and demonstration, of signs and inference, of the existence and nature of God and of cause, and of the reality of good and evil — arguing in each case that the concept is incoherent or the claim undecidable.

Crucially, Sextus was careful to distinguish the skeptic's procedure from a rival *dogmatism*. The skeptic does not positively *assert* that knowledge is impossible or that the dogmatists' claims are *false* — that would be to fall into dogmatism of the opposite kind (the error he attributed to the "Academic" skeptics). Rather, the Pyrrhonist merely *opposes* argument to argument, displays the equal force of contradictory positions, and thereby **suspends judgment**, making no positive claims of his own. His arguments are like a ladder to be kicked away, or a purge that flushes itself out along with the dogmatic disease. Through this immense critical labor, Sextus preserved a vast record of Hellenistic philosophy even as he sought to neutralize it, and profoundly influenced the revival of skepticism in the modern era, from Montaigne to Hume.

works.Outlines of Pyrrhonism:
The *Outlines of Pyrrhonism* (*Pyrrhōneioi Hypotypōseis*) is the most important and influential work of Sextus Empiricus and the single best surviving source for ancient **Pyrrhonist skepticism** — a systematic, relatively concise exposition of the skeptical way of life, its methods, and its aims. Written probably in the late second or early third century CE, it is the fullest ancient account we possess of a tradition that otherwise survives only in fragments, and it became the crucial text through which ancient skepticism was rediscovered and transmitted to the modern world.

The work is divided into three books. The **first book** lays out the general character of **Pyrrhonism**: what skepticism *is* (a *practice* of opposing appearances and arguments to one another so as to induce suspension of judgment), how it differs from dogmatism and from the negative dogmatism of the Academic skeptics, and its central concepts — **equipollence** (*isostheneia*), **suspension of judgment** (*epoché*), and the resulting **tranquility** (*ataraxia*). It also presents the famous skeptical **modes** or tropes (the Ten Modes of Aenesidemus and the Five Modes of Agrippa) that generate suspension of judgment, and carefully explains the skeptic's slogans ("no more this than that," "I suspend judgment") and how the skeptic can live and act without holding beliefs, by following appearances and custom.

The **second and third books** turn to a detailed **critique of the dogmatists**, systematically attacking their doctrines across the three parts of philosophy — logic (books II), physics, and ethics (book III) — showing that in each the dogmatists' claims can be opposed by equally strong counter-considerations. Clear, methodical, and comprehensive, the *Outlines* is the classic manual of Pyrrhonism, and its rediscovery in the sixteenth century (influencing Montaigne, Descartes, Bayle, and Hume) helped shape the entire course of modern philosophy.
works.Against the Logicians:
*Against the Logicians* is part of the larger work of Sextus Empiricus commonly known by the Latin title *Adversus Mathematicos* ("Against the Professors" or "Against the Mathematicians," in the sense of "learned experts") — a more extensive and detailed presentation of the skeptical arguments that Sextus had set out more concisely in his *Outlines of Pyrrhonism*. "Against the Logicians" comprises the two books devoted to attacking the dogmatic philosophers' theories of **logic and knowledge** (their "logical" or epistemological doctrines), and it is among the richest sources for ancient epistemology and skeptical argument.

In these books, Sextus mounts a thorough **critique of the theory of knowledge** as developed by the dogmatic schools, above all the Stoics and Epicureans. He examines and attacks the central concepts by which the dogmatists claimed that knowledge and truth are possible. He challenges the very notion of a **criterion of truth** — the supposed standard (whether reason, sense-perception, or the Stoic "cognitive impression") by which true judgments can be reliably distinguished from false ones — arguing that no such criterion can be established without circularity or infinite regress. He scrutinizes and undermines the dogmatic accounts of **truth** itself, of **sign** and **inference** (how we supposedly reason from the evident to the non-evident), and of **proof** or **demonstration**, contending that the concept of proof is incoherent and that nothing can ever be conclusively demonstrated.

Throughout, Sextus proceeds by his characteristic skeptical method: opposing argument to argument, exposing the assumptions and difficulties in the dogmatists' positions, and thereby driving toward **suspension of judgment** rather than any positive counter-doctrine. Because it preserves, in the course of refuting them, extensive and otherwise-lost details of Stoic and Epicurean epistemology and logic, *Against the Logicians* is doubly valuable — both as a monument of skeptical philosophy and as a crucial historical record of the very theories it seeks to overthrow. It profoundly shaped later debates about the foundations and limits of human knowledge.
